@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Danger Awareness
@graph.description: Some children have no internal alarm for danger. They don't recognize threats that others instinctively avoid. Here are 9 materials that help build the awareness that keeps them safe.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/safety skills / self-care / cognitive development / occupational therapy / psychology/danger awareness / safety judgment / risk perception / hazard recognition / self-protection skills/9-materials-that-help-with-danger-awareness#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my child has no sense of danger. it's like the part of the brain that says 'this could hurt me' just doesn't work. they'll run into a busy parking lot chasing a ball without a second thought. they'll reach toward a flame because it's pretty. they'll climb furniture that could topple, lean over balcony railings, approach aggressive dogs, and go with anyone who seems friendly. other children their age seem to instinctively pull back from danger — mine moves toward it or through it without any recognition of risk. i've tried explaining. i've shown them videos. i've used stern voices and consequences. nothing seems to create that internal alarm that other kids have naturally. they're not trying to be defiant — they genuinely don't perceive the threat. every outing is exhausting because i have to be their danger radar constantly. every environment has to be scanned and secured. every moment requires vigilance because they won't protect themselves. i'm not asking them to be anxious or fearful. i'm asking them to recognize that some things can hurt them and to respond accordingly. to stop before running into the street. to not touch things that are hot. to not go with strangers. basic survival awareness. how do i build danger recognition when it's simply not there? how do i teach them to see what could hurt them before it does?

acceptedAnswer.text: IMPAIRED DANGER AWARENESS / SAFETY JUDGMENT DEFICIT / RISK PERCEPTION DIFFICULTIES / HAZARD RECOGNITION DELAY! Danger awareness refers to the cognitive and perceptual ability to recognize potential threats in the environment and respond with appropriate caution or avoidance. Children with impaired danger awareness may have difficulty perceiving hazards, understanding cause-and-effect relationships between actions and harm, inhibiting impulses in the presence of danger, generalizing safety rules across contexts, or experiencing the typical fear response that protects most children. This can stem from developmental delays affecting abstract reasoning and prediction, sensory processing differences affecting threat perception, executive function deficits affecting impulse control, autism-related differences in threat processing, ADHD-related impulsivity overriding safety awareness, or intellectual disability affecting comprehension of danger concepts. Intervention focuses on explicit teaching of danger recognition, building automatic safety responses, and creating environmental protections while cognitive safety skills develop.

mainEntity.name: What is Impaired Danger Awareness / Safety Judgment Deficit / Risk Perception Difficulties / Hazard Recognition Delay?

acceptedAnswer.text: Danger awareness refers to the cognitive and perceptual ability to recognize potential threats in the environment and respond with appropriate caution or avoidance. Children with impaired danger awareness may have difficulty perceiving hazards, understanding cause-and-effect relationships between actions and harm, inhibiting impulses in the presence of danger, generalizing safety rules across contexts, or experiencing the typical fear response that protects most children. This can stem from developmental delays affecting abstract reasoning and prediction, sensory processing differences affecting threat perception, executive function deficits affecting impulse control, autism-related differences in threat processing, ADHD-related impulsivity overriding safety awareness, or intellectual disability affecting comprehension of danger concepts. Intervention focuses on explicit teaching of danger recognition, building automatic safety responses, and creating environmental protections while cognitive safety skills develop. Common signs include: Runs into streets, parking lots, or traffic areas without looking, Touches hot surfaces, sharp objects, or electrical outlets, Climbs to dangerous heights without fear, Approaches unfamiliar animals without caution, Goes with strangers willingly or doesn't recognize stranger danger.

mainEntity.name: How does a Safety Visual Cards (Safe vs. Dangerous) help with impaired danger awareness?

acceptedAnswer.text: Children with danger awareness deficits often can't categorize situations as safe or dangerous because they lack the perceptual framework for this distinction. Safety visual cards explicitly teach this categorization by presenting clear images of safe and dangerous situations side by side. The visual format bypasses verbal processing limitations and creates concrete, memorable images of danger. Cards typically show common hazards: hot stove (dangerous) vs. cold stove (safe), crossing with light (safe) vs. running into traffic (dangerous), known adult (safe) vs. stranger (dangerous). Repeated exposure builds pattern recognition. The child learns to identify visual cues that signal danger. Sorting activities (put cards in safe pile or dangerous pile) create active learning. This explicit teaching replaces the implicit learning that occurs naturally in typical development but may be impaired in children with developmental differences. Price: ₹200–800 (or DIY). DIY option: Create safety visual cards covering common danger categories: 1) Traffic safety: Crossing at crosswalk vs. running into street, looking both ways vs. not looking, waiting for car to stop vs. running in front. 2) Hot/cold: Stove with flame vs. stove off, hot water steam vs. cold tap, touching fire vs. staying back. 3) Heights: Standing safely vs. leaning over edge, using stairs with rail vs. climbing on furniture. 4) Stranger safety: Known trusted adult vs. unknown person, staying with caregiver vs. wandering with stranger. 5) Water safety: Pool with adult supervision vs. alone near water. 6) Animal safety: Gentle known pet vs. unfamiliar aggressive dog. Format: Clear photographs or realistic illustrations. One scenario per card. Color coding optional (red border = dangerous, green = safe). Laminate for durability. Use in sorting games, matching activities, and discussion. Safety: Cards teach recognition, not replace supervision. Always maintain appropriate supervision while teaching danger awareness.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Stories for Safety Situations help with impaired danger awareness?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social stories explain the 'why' and 'how' of safety in simple, personalized narratives. For children who don't intuitively understand danger, social stories explicitly teach what happens when we encounter hazards and what safe behavior looks like. Stories can cover specific scenarios the child struggles with: crossing the street, what to do if approached by a stranger, why we don't touch the stove. The narrative format is engaging and memorable. Stories can include cause-and-effect explanations (the stove is hot, touching it would hurt me, so I stay back), emotional context (Mommy would be sad if I got hurt), and clear behavioral scripts (when I get to a street, I stop, look, listen, then walk). Reading repeatedly builds the knowledge that typical children absorb through experience and observation. Personalized stories with the child's name and familiar settings are most effective. Price: ₹100–500 (or DIY). DIY option: Create personalized safety social stories: Structure: 1) Situation description: 'Sometimes I play outside near the street.' 2) Danger explanation: 'Cars drive on the street. They are big and fast. If a car hits me, I would get very hurt.' 3) Safe behavior: 'So I stay on the sidewalk. If I need to cross, I stop, look both ways, listen for cars, and walk when it's safe.' 4) Positive outcome: 'When I follow the safety rules, I stay safe and Mommy is happy.' 5) Affirmation: 'I can keep myself safe.' Create separate stories for each danger area: street safety, stranger safety, hot things, heights, water, animals. Use photos of the actual child and their environment when possible. Read daily, especially before relevant activities. Quiz gently: 'What do we do when we get to the street?' Safety: Stories supplement but don't replace supervision and environmental safety measures. Continue appropriate supervision while teaching.

mainEntity.name: How does a Stop Sign or Safety Symbol Cards help with impaired danger awareness?

acceptedAnswer.text: A stop sign is a universally recognized symbol that can be used to create an automatic response: when I see the stop sign, I STOP. For children with danger awareness deficits, this creates an external cue that compensates for missing internal alarm. Stop signs or other safety symbols (red hand, danger triangle) can be placed at hazard points in the environment: before the street, on the oven, near the pool gate. The child is taught a conditioned response: see sign → stop → check for safety. This automatic response can interrupt impulsive movement into danger. The visual cue provides the warning that the child's brain doesn't generate. Over time, the stop response may generalize — the child learns to stop and assess even without the physical sign. The simplicity of a single symbol makes it accessible even for children with cognitive limitations. Price: ₹50–300 (or DIY). DIY option: Create and use stop signs or safety symbols: 1) Make clear stop signs: red octagon with 'STOP' text, or red hand symbol. Size appropriate for placement location. 2) Place at danger points: door leading to street or parking lot, edge of driveway, near stove/oven, before stairs if falling is a risk, pool gate, balcony door. 3) Teach the response: 'When you see this sign, you STOP. Then you look for danger. Then you ask an adult or check if it's safe before going forward.' 4) Practice repeatedly in safe conditions. Approach sign → stop → model checking for safety → proceed safely. 5) Reinforce every time child stops at sign. Eventually practice: 'What should you do when you see a stop sign? Even when there's no sign, what should you do at the street?' Safety: Stop signs supplement but don't replace supervision or environmental barriers. Use physical barriers (locks, gates) for serious hazards.

mainEntity.name: How does a Cause-and-Effect Safety Games help with impaired danger awareness?

acceptedAnswer.text: Understanding danger requires understanding cause and effect: this action leads to that consequence. Children with danger awareness deficits often have difficulty with abstract cause-and-effect reasoning, especially for events they haven't directly experienced. Safety games make cause-and-effect concrete and interactive. Examples include: matching games (match the dangerous action to what happens — running into street matches picture of injury), sequencing games (put pictures in order: child touches hot stove → child's hand hurts → child cries), and consequence exploration games. Games can use puppets or dolls to demonstrate what happens when safety rules are broken without the child experiencing actual harm. The game format makes learning engaging rather than lecture-based. Repeated play builds the cause-and-effect understanding that underpins danger awareness. Price: ₹200–600 (or DIY). DIY option: Create cause-and-effect safety activities: 1) Matching game: Cards with dangerous actions (touching hot pan, running into street) paired with consequence cards (burned hand, car accident). Child matches action to result. Discuss: 'What happens when...?' 2) Sequencing: Picture sequences showing progression from dangerous action to consequence. Child puts in order and explains. 3) Puppet/doll demonstration: Use dolls to act out scenarios. 'Watch what happens when Teddy runs into the street without looking.' Discuss after. 4) 'What would happen if?' game: Present scenarios, child predicts outcome. 'What would happen if you touched the hot stove?' 5) Safe choice game: Present two options, child identifies which is safe and why. Focus on common dangers in child's environment. Keep consequences realistic but not traumatizing. Safety: Keep consequence depictions age-appropriate — realistic enough to convey danger, not traumatizing. Focus on learning, not fear induction.

mainEntity.name: How does a Safety Rule Posters for Key Locations help with impaired danger awareness?

acceptedAnswer.text: Children with danger awareness deficits benefit from environmental reminders that safety rules exist. Safety rule posters placed at key locations serve as constant visual reminders of expected safe behavior. A poster by the front door might show: 'STOP. Hold hands. Look for cars.' A poster in the kitchen might show: 'Don't touch stove. Tell adult if something is hot.' The poster makes the abstract rule concrete and ever-present. Unlike verbal reminders that happen once and are forgotten, posters provide continuous cueing. Pictures make rules accessible to pre-readers or children with language delays. Consistent placement at the relevant location strengthens the association: this location → these rules. Over time, seeing the poster triggers automatic recall of the safety behavior. Posters also prompt adults to review rules consistently at key moments. Price: ₹100–400 (or DIY). DIY option: Create location-specific safety rule posters: 1) Front door/outdoor exit: 'STOP. Hold adult's hand. Look for cars before walking.' with pictures. 2) Kitchen: 'Hot things can hurt. Don't touch stove. Ask adult for help.' 3) Bathroom: 'Water can be hot. Test with adult. No running — slippery floor.' 4) Pool/water area: 'STOP. Wait for adult. Never go in water alone.' 5) Car/parking lot: 'Hold hands. Walk, don't run. Watch for moving cars.' Include: Simple language, clear pictures demonstrating rule, prominent placement at child's eye level. Review poster rules regularly. Point to poster before relevant activities: 'Let's look at our outside safety rules before we go out.' Laminate for durability. Safety: Posters remind but don't enforce. Maintain supervision and physical barriers for serious hazards.

mainEntity.name: How does a Video Modeling for Safety Behaviors help with impaired danger awareness?

acceptedAnswer.text: Video modeling shows correct safety behavior in action, allowing children to observe and imitate safe responses to dangerous situations. For children with danger awareness deficits, video provides clear demonstration of what to do — stopping at a street, looking both ways, waiting for cars. Videos can show both safe behavior and unsafe behavior with consequences (age-appropriately), helping children distinguish between them. The visual medium is engaging and can be replayed repeatedly. Children often attend better to video than to verbal instruction. Video modeling can cover scenarios that are difficult to practice safely in real life — what to do if a stranger approaches, what to do if you see fire. Self-modeling (videos of the child themselves demonstrating safe behavior) is particularly powerful for building confidence and skill. Price: ₹0–500 (using existing devices). DIY option: Create or curate safety videos: 1) Model videos: Film trusted adults or peers demonstrating safe behaviors — stopping at street, looking both ways, crossing safely. Narrate the steps. 2) Self-modeling: Film the child successfully completing safety behaviors during practice. Watch together with praise. 3) Comparison videos: Show unsafe vs. safe behavior (age-appropriately) — point out the difference. 4) Curated content: Age-appropriate safety videos from educational sources (preview first for appropriateness). Topics to cover: street/traffic safety, stranger safety, fire safety, water safety, hot object safety. Watch repeatedly before relevant activities. Pause and discuss: 'What did they do? Why?' Practice immediately after watching when possible. Safety: Preview all videos for age-appropriateness. Avoid content that may traumatize rather than teach.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/safety skills / self-care / cognitive development / occupational therapy / psychology/danger awareness / safety judgment / risk perception / hazard recognition / self-protection skills/9-materials-that-help-with-danger-awareness#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Impaired Danger Awareness
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Danger awareness deficits are safety-critical and require a layered approach: explicit teaching to build recognition, repeated practice to build automatic responses, AND environmental modification to provide protection while skills develop. Teaching alone is insufficient — the environment must be modified to protect children whose danger perception is unreliable. The goal is building internal danger recognition that generalizes across settings, but this takes time and consistent intervention. Never rely solely on teaching; always maintain appropriate supervision and physical barriers for serious hazards.

supply.name: Stop Sign or Safety Symbol Cards

Intervention focuses on explicit teaching of danger recognition, building automatic safety responses, and creating environmental protections while cognitive safety skills develop. Stop signs or other safety symbols (red hand, danger triangle) can be placed at hazard points in the environment: before the street, on the oven, near the pool gate. The child is taught a conditioned response: see sign → stop → check for safety. This automatic response can interrupt impulsive movement into danger. The visual cue provides the warning that the child's brain doesn't generate. Over time, the stop response may generalize — the child learns to stop and assess even without the physical sign. The simplicity of a single symbol makes it accessible even for children with cognitive limitations.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create and use stop signs or safety symbols: 1) Make clear stop signs: red octagon with 'STOP' text, or red hand symbol. Size appropriate for placement location. 2) Place at danger points: door leading to street or parking lot, edge of driveway, near stove/oven, before stairs if falling is a risk, pool gate, balcony door. 3) Teach the response: 'When you see this sign, you STOP. Then you look for danger. Then you ask an adult or check if it's safe before going forward.' 4) Practice repeatedly in safe conditions. Approach sign → stop → model checking for safety → proceed safely. 5) Reinforce every time child stops at sign. Eventually practice: 'What should you do when you see a stop sign? Even when there's no sign, what should you do at the street?'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Stop signs supplement but don't replace supervision or environmental barriers. Use physical barriers (locks, gates) for serious hazards.

item.name: Cause-and-Effect Safety Games
item.description: Understanding danger requires understanding cause and effect: this action leads to that consequence. Children with danger awareness deficits often have difficulty with abstract cause-and-effect reasoning, especially for events they haven't directly experienced. Safety games make cause-and-effect concrete and interactive. Examples include: matching games (match the dangerous action to what happens — running into street matches picture of injury), sequencing games (put pictures in order: child touches hot stove → child's hand hurts → child cries), and consequence exploration games. Games can use puppets or dolls to demonstrate what happens when safety rules are broken without the child experiencing actual harm. The game format makes learning engaging rather than lecture-based. Repeated play builds the cause-and-effect understanding that underpins danger awareness.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create cause-and-effect safety activities: 1) Matching game: Cards with dangerous actions (touching hot pan, running into street) paired with consequence cards (burned hand, car accident). Child matches action to result. Discuss: 'What happens when...?' 2) Sequencing: Picture sequences showing progression from dangerous action to consequence. Child puts in order and explains. 3) Puppet/doll demonstration: Use dolls to act out scenarios. 'Watch what happens when Teddy runs into the street without looking.' Discuss after. 4) 'What would happen if?' game: Present scenarios, child predicts outcome. 'What would happen if you touched the hot stove?' 5) Safe choice game: Present two options, child identifies which is safe and why. Focus on common dangers in child's environment. Keep consequences realistic but not traumatizing.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep consequence depictions age-appropriate — realistic enough to convey danger, not traumatizing. Focus on learning, not fear induction.

item.name: Safety Rule Posters for Key Locations
item.description: Children with danger awareness deficits benefit from environmental reminders that safety rules exist. Safety rule posters placed at key locations serve as constant visual reminders of expected safe behavior. A poster by the front door might show: 'STOP. Hold hands. Look for cars.' A poster in the kitchen might show: 'Don't touch stove. Tell adult if something is hot.' The poster makes the abstract rule concrete and ever-present. Unlike verbal reminders that happen once and are forgotten, posters provide continuous cueing. Pictures make rules accessible to pre-readers or children with language delays. Consistent placement at the relevant location strengthens the association: this location → these rules. Over time, seeing the poster triggers automatic recall of the safety behavior. Posters also prompt adults to review rules consistently at key moments.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create location-specific safety rule posters: 1) Front door/outdoor exit: 'STOP. Hold adult's hand. Look for cars before walking.' with pictures. 2) Kitchen: 'Hot things can hurt. Don't touch stove. Ask adult for help.' 3) Bathroom: 'Water can be hot. Test with adult. No running — slippery floor.' 4) Pool/water area: 'STOP. Wait for adult. Never go in water alone.' 5) Car/parking lot: 'Hold hands. Walk, don't run. Watch for moving cars.' Include: Simple language, clear pictures demonstrating rule, prominent placement at child's eye level. Review poster rules regularly. Point to poster before relevant activities: 'Let's look at our outside safety rules before we go out.' Laminate for durability.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Posters remind but don't enforce. Maintain supervision and physical barriers for serious hazards.

item.name: Video Modeling for Safety Behaviors
item.description: Video modeling shows correct safety behavior in action, allowing children to observe and imitate safe responses to dangerous situations. For children with danger awareness deficits, video provides clear demonstration of what to do — stopping at a street, looking both ways, waiting for cars. Videos can show both safe behavior and unsafe behavior with consequences (age-appropriately), helping children distinguish between them. The visual medium is engaging and can be replayed repeatedly. Children often attend better to video than to verbal instruction. Video modeling can cover scenarios that are difficult to practice safely in real life — what to do if a stranger approaches, what to do if you see fire. Self-modeling (videos of the child themselves demonstrating safe behavior) is particularly powerful for building confidence and skill.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create or curate safety videos: 1) Model videos: Film trusted adults or peers demonstrating safe behaviors — stopping at street, looking both ways, crossing safely. Narrate the steps. 2) Self-modeling: Film the child successfully completing safety behaviors during practice. Watch together with praise. 3) Comparison videos: Show unsafe vs. safe behavior (age-appropriately) — point out the difference. 4) Curated content: Age-appropriate safety videos from educational sources (preview first for appropriateness). Topics to cover: street/traffic safety, stranger safety, fire safety, water safety, hot object safety. Watch repeatedly before relevant activities. Pause and discuss: 'What did they do? Why?' Practice immediately after watching when possible.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Preview all videos for age-appropriateness. Avoid content that may traumatize rather than teach.

item.name: Safety Response Practice Cards
item.description: Knowing about danger isn't enough — children need automatic responses that activate when danger is present. Safety response practice cards provide scenarios for practicing 'What would you do if...?' responses until they become automatic. Cards present a situation (a stranger asks you to come with them; you see a fire; you're at the pool and no adult is watching) and the child must verbalize or demonstrate the safe response. Repeated practice builds automatic responding. The card format makes practice portable and easy to incorporate into daily routines. Practice can be gamified — child earns points for correct responses. Cards can progress from simple (What do you do at a stop sign?) to complex (What do you do if a stranger offers you candy and says your mom said it's okay?). The goal is building responses that activate without conscious deliberation.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create safety response practice cards: Format: Scenario on one side, correct response on back for reference. Categories: 1) Traffic: 'You're playing and your ball rolls into the street. What do you do?' (Stop. Don't chase it. Ask adult.) 2) Strangers: 'Someone you don't know offers you candy. What do you do?' (Say no. Don't take it. Find trusted adult.) 3) Fire/heat: 'You smell smoke in the house. What do you do?' (Get out. Tell adult. Don't hide.) 4) Water: 'You're near the pool and no adult is watching. What do you do?' (Move away from water. Find adult.) 5) Getting lost: 'You can't find your parent at the store. What do you do?' (Stay where you are. Find store worker. Don't leave with stranger.) Practice regularly — daily is ideal. Celebrate correct responses. For wrong answers, teach the correct response and practice again.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Practice builds skill, but young children still need supervision. Practice doesn't guarantee safe behavior in real situations.

item.name: Body Awareness and Boundary Tools
item.description: Danger awareness includes awareness of personal boundaries — understanding that certain touches are not okay, that personal space matters, and that one's body deserves protection. Body awareness tools teach children about safe vs. unsafe touch, body autonomy, and the right to say no. This aspect of danger awareness protects against abuse and exploitation, which children with developmental differences are at elevated risk for. Tools include: body maps showing private areas, social stories about safe and unsafe touch, dolls for demonstrating boundaries, and practice scripts for saying 'no' and telling a trusted adult. Building body awareness also supports general safety — children who feel ownership of their bodies may be more likely to protect them from all types of harm.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use body awareness and boundary teaching: 1) Body map: Simple outline of body showing private areas (bathing suit areas) — 'These parts are private. No one should touch them except doctor with parent present, or parent helping with hygiene.' 2) Safe vs. unsafe touch social story: Explain the difference between safe touch (hug from parent, doctor exam with parent) and unsafe touch. Teach that unsafe touch is never the child's fault and should always be told to trusted adult. 3) Saying no practice: Role play scenarios where child practices saying 'No' and 'Stop' firmly, then telling a trusted adult. 4) Trusted adult identification: Help child identify 2-3 trusted adults they can tell if something unsafe happens. 5) Secrets vs. surprises: Teach that safe secrets are happy (surprise party) and unsafe secrets make you uncomfortable — unsafe secrets should always be told. Use age-appropriate language. Revisit regularly.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: This content should be taught in age-appropriate ways. Consider professional guidance if the child has trauma history. This teaching is about empowerment, not fear.

item.name: Environmental Safety Modifications
item.description: While teaching danger awareness, environmental modifications protect children whose recognition and response skills are still developing. Locks, gates, alarms, and barriers provide physical protection that doesn't rely on the child's judgment. This isn't giving up on teaching — it's providing a safety net while skills develop. Modifications include: door alarms that alert when child exits, pool fencing with locked gates, stove knob covers or guards, cabinet locks for dangerous items, window locks to prevent falls, GPS trackers for children who wander. The environment becomes an ally in protection. Modifications should be regularly reviewed as child develops — some can be removed as danger awareness improves. For children with significant danger awareness deficits, environmental modifications may remain necessary long-term as a layer of protection alongside skill teaching.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implement environmental safety modifications: 1) Door/exit alarms: Chimes or alarms that alert when doors open, especially to outside areas. 2) Locks: High locks on exterior doors child can't reach. Pool gate locks. Cabinet locks for dangerous items (chemicals, medicines, sharp objects). 3) Stove safety: Stove knob covers, stove guard/shield, removing knobs when not in use. 4) Window safety: Window locks limiting opening, window guards to prevent falls. 5) Water safety: Pool fence with self-closing, self-latching gate. Toilet locks if child is drawn to water. 6) Tracking: GPS tracker in shoe, clothing, or wearable for children who wander. 7) Visibility: Remove hiding spots that make supervision difficult. Prioritize modifications based on child's specific danger behaviors. Start with highest-risk areas. Review regularly and adjust as child develops.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Environmental modifications are essential protection, not substitute for supervision. Use layered protection: teaching + modification + supervision.

socialMedia.caption: When they don't see what could hurt them. Some children have no internal danger alarm — they run into streets, touch hot things, and approach strangers without caution. Here are 9 materials that help build awareness: visual safe/dangerous cards, safety social stories, stop signs at danger points, cause-effect games, safety rule posters, video modeling, response practice cards, body awareness tools, and environmental modifications. Build the awareness that keeps them safe. 🔖 #DangerAwareness #ChildSafety #PinnacleBlooms
